%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Section Objective:** Understand how to expand a proper rational function into partial fractions using the Method of Clearing Fractions, and recognize when and how to apply it.

When a rational function \(F(x) = P(x)/Q(x)\) is proper (degree of numerator less than degree of denominator), it can be broken into simpler pieces called **partial fractions**. The **Method of Clearing Fractions** is the most general technique for finding the unknown coefficients in that expansion. By multiplying both sides of the equation by the full denominator \(Q(x)\), you eliminate all fractions and reduce the problem to matching polynomial coefficients or substituting convenient values of \(x\). After this section, you will be able to set up and solve for every coefficient in a partial fraction expansion, even when the denominator has repeated factors.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The three-step flow of the Method of Clearing Fractions: write the template, multiply through by \(Q(x)\), then solve for each coefficient by substitution or coefficient matching.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215429960-5888.png)

## Core Idea

The **Method of Clearing Fractions** works in three stages.

**Stage 1 — Write the template.** Assume \(F(x)\) equals a sum of partial fractions whose form depends on the factors of \(Q(x)\). A distinct linear factor \((x - a)\) contributes one term \(k/(x-a)\); a repeated factor \((x-a)^r\) contributes \(r\) terms: \(k_1/(x-a) + k_2/(x-a)^2 + \cdots + k_r/(x-a)^r\).

**Stage 2 — Clear the fractions.** Multiply both sides by the full denominator \(Q(x)\). Every fraction cancels, leaving a polynomial identity with the unknowns \(k_1, k_2, \ldots\) on the right.

**Stage 3 — Solve.** Either substitute convenient values of \(x\) (e.g., the roots of \(Q(x)\)) to isolate individual \(k\) values, or expand both sides and match coefficients of equal powers of \(x\).

### COMMON EXAM TRAP

A repeated factor \((x+3)^2\) **must** generate two separate terms. Writing only one term for a repeated factor is the single most common error on partial fraction problems.

**Minimal example:** For \(F(x) = \frac{2x+1}{(x+1)(x+2)}\), write \(F(x) = \frac{k_1}{x+1} + \frac{k_2}{x+2}\), multiply through by \((x+1)(x+2)\), then substitute \(x = -1\) and \(x = -2\) to find \(k_1\) and \(k_2\) immediately.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Write a partial fraction template matching every factor and its multiplicity in \(Q(x)\).
- Multiply both sides by \(Q(x)\) to clear all fractions and get a polynomial identity.
- Repeated factor \((x-a)^r\) requires \(r\) separate terms — missing one is the classic trap.

*In the next section (B.5-2), we meet the Heaviside Cover-Up Method, a faster shortcut that works whenever all factors of \(Q(x)\) are distinct and non-repeated.*
